result:
Floating ribs wake me at 3:17 a.m.—the precise moment when darkness feels like warm graphite, pressing into the fibers of the mattress. I open my eyes and the room is still, the same motionless aquarium it was when I surrendered to sleep. Only now the air itself is humming, thinner, like the roof has quietly drifted up and left me several inches higher than I should be. Outside, frost folds over the azalea hedge; the garden floats inside a frail glaze of moon.

I lie there, one heartbeat old, one entire lifetime wide. A lifetime is a loop, after all, not a line. My grandfather used to say this when he buffed his shoes on a Sunday before church, as if calculus and instructions were etched inside the skin of brown leather. “Time only ticks because we haven’t figured out how to listen to it,” he’d murmur. He died at ninety-three with a fountain pen clipped inside his shirt pocket, ink removed years earlier so that gravity would not pull it downward when he reclined. His body remained aloft, in a way that pen would not have managed. My mother said he used to clip watches to the inside of his coat so no one could steal the ticking noise. She was being whimsical; the watches were all broken, but he wore them like lost birds nesting against the lining of his chest. In dreams, I still wind them.

The fridge kicks on—a low, mammalian growl. Somewhere down the spinal corridor of the house, copper pipes expand and contract. A few ounces of vaporous city-steam spill from the radiator valve, smelling of crayons and forgotten homework. I once interviewed a subway songster stationed beneath 86th and Lexington. His harmonica keys had corroded green from transit exhaust. He played only in diminished sevenths: the sound of things leaving. People dropped coins into his hat as apology. Music is often apology, he said, and took out a small photo: a child’s face obscured by the bright fugue of a laundry soap bubble. The paper was soft as a pressed violet. I asked if the bubble had popped. He shrugged. “All photos survive the bubbles inside them,” he replied. Then the downtown 6 arrived with its brass-band thunder, and we dissolved in its wash.

Memory parks in cul-de-sacs we think are going somewhere. I navigate one now: the wallpaper from our first apartment—pink flamingos wearing scarves—how it would ripple in August heat, the adhesive surrendering like faith. My wife used to iron curtains while listening to weather satellites on shortwave radio, waiting for the Czech announcer who would pronounce “thunderstorm” as if it tasted of marzipan. We owned three mismatched spoons at first; one was bent. We decided the bent one was luckier because it had failed gracefully. Kitchen conversations went: what shape is luck? Parabolic, she claimed. I said tubular, something water runs through. In the end we eased our syllables around a shared imaginary cup until it overflowed.

Cup of. Cup of what? Tea, remorse, equations. Years later, on a ferry crossing the mouth of the Saguenay, we leaned against the salt rail and watched whales knot themselves into single silver signatures beneath the vessel. A Spanish tourist dotted rosary beads against his lip. Spray dashed our faces; it tasted metallic, near betrayal. I felt how water can be older than stone and infinitely less patient. She wore a green cardigan. The sleeves kept falling past her hands. Each time she shrugged them back, it looked like surrender to a miniature and private weather. I wanted to write the scene down immediately, but my phone had fluttered dead from cold. Paper had I none. So I did the only thing capable of inscription: I opened my mouth underwater. Not literally—my mouth stayed shut, but somewhere beneath the sternum the black ink of the moment squirted into chambers normally reserved for blood. Heart as inkwell. The whales breached and fell back like the slow thoughts of a sleeping continent. I realized: we breathe best inside parentheses. Everything else happens outside the brackets and comes at us too sharp-edged. The parentheses are where the milk is sweetest, where the infant universe sucks its thumb.

Dawn begins to smudge the pane. First exposure is always grey-lavender, then peach, then the ordinary betrayal of blue. I once photographed the same window for an entire year. At 6:11 a.m. every day, I pressed the shutter. I labeled the images only by shutter speed: 1/80 1/80 1/60 1/60 1/125. Months later, I laid the prints across the living-room floor like tarot. They whispered the speed of light at which regret accumulates. Neighbors thought I’d lost time itself; I still can’t decide if they were right.

A single sparrow lands on the fire escape. It holds a twig the length of its own body. The twig is forked at the tip—like an unstruck match—like yesterday’s indecision. The bird studies the closed windowpane; sees its own reflection; rehearses the battle it won’t commence. Then with pure disdain flings the twig downward. The fragment cartwheels through the iron ribs, clacks twice against bricks, is gone. Message received: architecture is a temporary misunderstanding between sky and ground. We scaffold our lives within it. I once stood below the half-built arch of a bridge in Ljubljana. A welder above me showered orange blossoms. Each blossom cooled into ash before it reached the river. I thought: whole marriages arc above us like this, incandescent before they cool. Yet the river doesn’t mind; it’s already full of shadows.

My shoulder aches because the pillow has surrendered its loft and memory foam has forgotten us. Despite marketing, nothing truly remembers; it merely reenacts choreography. The curtain’s hem begins to lift from floorboard drafts. Light bleeds through warp and weft: a Morse made of thread. I translate: creatures, leave your burrows. The slant rhyme between fear and morning is obsolete. By this longitude, the streetlamps click off; I hear them die in sequence down the block—expiring relays. A garbage truck grinds forward, hydraulic jaws compressing the disposable prophecies of our kitchen bags. Somewhere a child cries because cartoons have not yet begun. Somewhere a cat steals across the skylight like a comma giving up.

You ask—metaphorically—where the thousand words will land. Maybe in the hollow of the throat, where pulse chisels every second into echo. Maybe at the hinge of the mouth that once tasted fog in San Francisco before it carried prohibition against adjectives. Maybe here, still horizontal, unwilling to surrender the quilt’s gray geography of me. I inch my foot toward cold air, then retreat. The minute is taffy. I stretch it; it springs back. This is how minutes behave when they discover they have nowhere else to be.

A final confession: I have always believed the universe was shaped like a paper boat launched on a rainy curb; it would eventually soak, sink, and swirl through grates into subterranean rivers. The boat carried tiny pellets of crimped handwriting—shopping lists, disavowals, doodles of the impossible house I still draw (round windows, spiral staircase descending into a well of sound). On the underside of each paper hull, you’d find coordinates to the last dream I forgot upon waking. Last night—or earlier this morning—there was music emanating from storm drains. Somebody down there tuned bicycle spokes to resonate with distant thunderstorms. Such symphonies enact a gentle blackmail: listen forever or feel the absence forever. My paper boat navigates the downpour, rigged to a single feather. In sleep I wave my ink-stained fingertips to the silhouettes leaning over grates. Good morning. Goodbye. The feather is hope, I insist. The feather is punctuation, they answer. Who is right is still en route.

Here at 986 words, the radiator settles like an old dog collapsing on its flank. The mirror across the room holds a displaced me—rumpled hair, eyes the grey of wet sidewalk chalk. Tomorrow’s obligations congeal in cubicles two boroughs away. There will be emails about synergies, early leaves curling from an overwatered pothos perched on the copier. And yet already that tomorrow exists at least three hours away from being born. And I can still retract the wish to be useful, replace it with the wish to be porous so songs travel through unimpeded.

Word 1000 is spelled dawn.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
